lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Gloucester, ’tis true that we are in great danger;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The greater therefore should our courage be.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Good morrow, brother Bedford. God Almighty!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: There is some soul of goodness in things evil,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Would men observingly distil it out.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: For our bad neighbour makes us early stirrers,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Which is both healthful and good husbandry:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Besides, they are our outward consciences,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And preachers to us all, admonishing

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: That we should dress us fairly for our end.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Thus may we gather honey from the weed,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And make a moral of the devil himself.

lines.speaker: ERPINGHAM
lines.text: Not so, my liege: this lodging likes me better,

lines.speaker: ERPINGHAM
lines.text: Since I may say “Now lie I like a king.”

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: ’Tis good for men to love their present pains

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Upon example; so the spirit is eased:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And when the mind is quicken’d, out of doubt,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The organs, though defunct and dead before,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Break up their drowsy grave and newly move,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: With casted slough and fresh legerity.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Lend me thy cloak, Sir Thomas. Brothers both,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Commend me to the princes in our camp;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Do my good morrow to them, and anon

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Desire them all to my pavilion.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: We shall, my liege.

lines.speaker: ERPINGHAM
lines.text: Shall I attend your grace?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: No, my good knight;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Go with my brothers to my lords of England:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I and my bosom must debate a while,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And then I would no other company.

lines.speaker: ERPINGHAM
lines.text: The Lord in heaven bless thee, noble Harry!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: God-a-mercy, old heart! thou speak’st cheerfully.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Qui vas là?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: A friend.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Discuss unto me; art thou officer?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Or art thou base, common and popular?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I am a gentleman of a company.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Trail’st thou the puissant pike?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Even so. What are you?

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: As good a gentleman as the emperor.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Then you are a better than the king.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: The king’s a bawcock, and a heart of gold,

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: A lad of life, an imp of fame;

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Of parents good, of fist most valiant:

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: I kiss his dirty shoe, and from heart-string

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: I love the lovely bully. What is thy name?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Harry le Roy.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Le Roy! a Cornish name: art thou of Cornish crew?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: No, I am a Welshman.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Know’st thou Fluellen?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Yes.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Tell him I’ll knock his leek about his pate

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Upon Saint Davy’s day.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Do not you wear your dagger in your cap that day, lest

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: he knock that about yours.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: Art thou his friend?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And his kinsman too.

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: The figo for thee, then!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I thank you: God be with you!

lines.speaker: PISTOL
lines.text: My name is Pistol call’d.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: It sorts well with your fierceness.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Captain Fluellen!

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: So! in the name of Jesu Christ, speak lower. It is the

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: greatest admiration in the universal world, when the true and aunchient prerogatifes

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: and laws of the wars is not kept: if you would take

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: the pains but to examine the wars of Pompey the Great, you

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: shall find, I warrant you, that there is no tiddle taddle nor

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: pibble pabble in Pompey’s camp; I warrant you, you shall find the

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: ceremonies of the wars, and the cares of it, and the forms

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: of it, and the sobriety of it, and the modesty of it,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: to be otherwise.

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: Why, the enemy is loud; you hear him all night.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: If the enemy is an ass and a fool and a prating

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: coxcomb, is it meet, think you, that we should also, look you,

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: be an ass and a fool and a prating coxcomb? in your

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: own conscience, now?

lines.speaker: GOWER
lines.text: I will speak lower.

lines.speaker: FLUELLEN
lines.text: I pray you and beseech you that you will.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Though it appear a little out of fashion,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: There is much care and valour in this Welshman.

lines.speaker: COURT
lines.text: Brother John Bates, is not that the morning which breaks yonder?

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: I think it be: but we have no great cause to desire

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: the approach of day.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: We see yonder the beginning of the day, but I think we

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: shall never see the end of it. Who goes there?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: A friend.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Under what captain serve you?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Under Sir Thomas Erpingham.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: A good old commander and a most kind gentleman: I pray you,

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: what thinks he of our estate?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Even as men wrecked upon a sand, that look to be washed

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: off the next tide.

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: He hath not told his thought to the king?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: No; nor it is not meet he should. For, though I speak

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: it to you, I think the king is but a man, as

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I am: the violet smells to him as it doth to me;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: the element shows to him as it doth to me; all his

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: senses have but human conditions: his ceremonies laid by, in his nakedness

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: he appears but a man; and though his affections are higher mounted

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: than ours, yet, when they stoop, they stoop with the like wing.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Therefore when he sees reason of fears, as we do, his fears,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: out of doubt, be of the same relish as ours are: yet,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: in reason, no man should possess him with any appearance of fear,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: lest he, by showing it, should dishearten his army.

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: He may show what outward courage he will; but I believe, as

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: cold a night as ’tis, he could wish himself in Thames up

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: to the neck; and so I would he were, and I by

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: him, at all adventures, so we were quit here.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: By my troth, I will speak my conscience of the King: I

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: think he would not wish himself anywhere but where he is.

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: Then I would he were here alone; so should he be sure

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: to be ransomed, and a many poor men’s lives saved.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I dare say you love him not so ill, to wish him

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: here alone, howsoever you speak this to feel other men’s minds: methinks

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I could not die anywhere so contented as in the king’s company;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: his cause being just and his quarrel honourable.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: That’s more than we know.

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: Ay, or more than we should seek after; for we know enough,

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: if we know we are the king’s subjects: if his cause be

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: wrong, our obedience to the king wipes the crime of it out

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: of us.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: But if the cause be not good, the king himself hath a

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: heavy reckoning to make, when all those legs and arms and heads,

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: chopped off in a battle, shall join together at the latter day

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: and cry all “We died at such a place;” some swearing, some

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: crying for a surgeon, some upon their wives left poor behind them,

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: some upon the debts they owe, some upon their children rawly left.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: I am afeard there are few die well that die in a

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: battle; for how can they charitably dispose of anything, when blood is

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: their argument? Now, if these men do not die well, it will

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: be a black matter for the king that led them to it;

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: who to disobey were against all proportion of subjection.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: So, if a son that is by his father sent about merchandise

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: do sinfully miscarry upon the sea, the imputation of his wickedness, by

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: your rule, should be imposed upon his father that sent him: or

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: if a servant, under his master’s command transporting a sum of money,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: be assailed by robbers and die in many irreconcil’d iniquities, you may

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: call the business of the master the author of the servant’s damnation:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: but this is not so: the king is not bound to answer

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: the particular endings of his soldiers, the father of his son, nor

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: the master of his servant; for they purpose not their death, when

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: they purpose their services. Besides, there is no king, be his cause

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: never so spotless, if it come to the arbitrement of swords, can

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: try it out with all unspotted soldiers: some peradventure have on them

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: the guilt of premeditated and contrived murder; some, of beguiling virgins with

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: the broken seals of perjury; some, making the wars their bulwark, that

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: have before gored the gentle bosom of peace with pillage and robbery.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Now, if these men have defeated the law and outrun native punishment,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: though they can outstrip men, they have no wings to fly from

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: God: war is his beadle, war is his vengeance; so that here

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: men are punished for before-breach of the king’s laws in now the

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: king’s quarrel: where they feared the death, they have borne life away;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: and where they would be safe, they perish: then if they die

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: unprovided, no more is the king guilty of their damnation than he

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: was before guilty of those impieties for the which they are now

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: visited. Every subject’s duty is the king’s; but every subject’s soul is

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: his own. Therefore should every soldier in the wars do as every

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: sick man in his bed, wash every mote out of his conscience:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: and dying so, death is to him advantage; or not dying, the

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: time was blessedly lost wherein such preparation was gained: and in him

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: that escapes, it were not sin to think that, making God so

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: free an offer, He let him outlive that day to see His

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: greatness and to teach others how they should prepare.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: ’Tis certain, every man that dies ill, the ill upon his own

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: head, the king is not to answer for it.

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: I do not desire he should answer for me; and yet I

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: determine to fight lustily for him.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I myself heard the king say he would not be ransomed.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Ay, he said so, to make us fight cheerfully: but when our

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: throats are cut, he may be ransomed, and we ne’er the wiser.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If I live to see it, I will never trust his word after.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: You pay him then. That’s a perilous shot out of an elder-gun,

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: that a poor and a private displeasure can do against a monarch!

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: you may as well go about to turn the sun to ice

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: with fanning in his face with a peacock’s feather. You’ll never trust

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: his word after! come, ’tis a foolish saying.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Your reproof is something too round: I should be angry with you,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: if the time were convenient.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Let it be a quarrel between us, if you live.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I embrace it.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: How shall I know thee again?

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Give me any gage of thine, and I will wear it in

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: my bonnet: then, if ever thou darest acknowledge it, I will make

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: it my quarrel.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Here’s my glove: give me another of thine.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: There.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: This will I also wear in my cap: if ever thou come

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: to me and say, after tomorrow, “This is my glove,” by this

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: hand I will take thee a box on the ear.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: If ever I live to see it, I will challenge it.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Thou darest as well be hane’d.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Well, I will do it, though I take thee in the king’s company.

lines.speaker: WILLIAMS
lines.text: Keep thy word: fare thee well.

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: Be friends, you English fools, be friends: we have French quarrels enow,

lines.speaker: BATES
lines.text: if you could tell how to reckon.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Indeed, the French may lay twenty French crowns to one, they will

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: beat us; for they bear them on their shoulders: but it is

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: no English treason to cut French crowns, and tomorrow the king himself

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: will be a clipper.

lines.speaker: ERPINGHAM
lines.text: My lord, your nobles, jealous of your absence,

lines.speaker: ERPINGHAM
lines.text: Seek through your camp to find you.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Good old knight,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Collect them all together at my tent:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I’ll be before thee.

lines.speaker: ERPINGHAM
lines.text: I shall do’t, my lord.

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: O God of battles! steel my soldiers’ hearts;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Possess them not with fear; take from them now

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The sense of reckoning, if the opposed numbers

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Pluck their hearts from them. Not today, O Lord,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: O, not today, think not upon the fault

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My father made in compassing the crown!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I Richard’s body have interred new;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: And on it have bestow’d more contrite tears

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Than from it issued forced drops of blood:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Five hundred poor I have in yearly pay,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Who twice a day their wither’d hands hold up

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Toward heaven, to pardon blood; and I have built

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Two chantries, where the sad and solemn priests

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Sing still for Richard’s soul. More will I do;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Though all that I can do is nothing worth,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Since that my penitence comes after all,

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: Imploring pardon.

lines.speaker: GLOUCESTER
lines.text: My liege!

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: My brother Gloucester’s voice? Ay;

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: I know thy errand, I will go with thee:

lines.speaker: KING HENRY
lines.text: The day, my friends and all things stay for me.

stage_directions.text: Enter King Henry, Bedford, and Gloucester.
Good morrow, old Sir Thomas Erpingham: A good soft pillow for that good white head Were better than a churlish turf of France.
Enter three soldiers, John Bates, Alexander Court, and Michael Williams.

Upon the king! let us our lives, our souls, Our debts, our careful wives, Our children and our sins lay on the king! We must bear all. O hard condition, Twin-born with greatness, subject to the breath Of every fool, whose sense no more can feel But his own wringing! What infinite heart’s-ease Must kings neglect, that private men enjoy! And what have kings, that privates have not too, Save ceremony, save general ceremony? And what art thou, thou idol ceremony? What kind of god art thou, that suffer’st more Of mortal griefs than do thy worshippers? What are thy rents? what are thy comings in? O ceremony, show me but thy worth! What is thy soul of adoration? Art thou aught else but place, degree and form, Creating awe and fear in other men? Wherein thou art less happy being fear’d Than they in fearing. What drink’st thou oft, instead of homage sweet, But poison’d flattery? O, be sick, great greatness, And bid thy ceremony give thee cure! Think’st thou the fiery fever will go out With titles blown from adulation? Will it give place to flexure and low bending? Canst thou, when thou command’st the beggar’s knee, Command the health of it? No, thou proud dream, That play’st so subtly with a king’s repose; I am a king that find thee, and I know ’Tis not the balm, the sceptre and the ball, The sword, the mace, the crown imperial, The intertissued robe of gold and pearl, The farced title running ’fore the king, The throne he sits on, nor the tide of pomp That beats upon the high shore of this world, No, not all these, thrice-gorgeous ceremony, Not all these, laid in bed majestical, Can sleep so soundly as the wretched slave, Who with a body fill’d and vacant mind Gets him to rest, cramm’d with distressful bread; Never sees horrid night, the child of hell, But, like a lackey, from the rise to set Sweats in the eye of Phoebus and all night Sleeps in Elysium; next day after dawn, Doth rise and help Hyperion to his horse, And follows so the ever-running year, With profitable labour, to his grave: And, but for ceremony, such a wretch, Winding up days with toil and nights with sleep, Had the forehand and vantage of a king. The slave, a member of the country’s peace, Enjoys it; but in gross brain little wots What watch the king keeps to maintain the peace, Whose hours the peasant best advantages.

glosses.phrase: some soul of goodness in things evil
glosses.definition: even in bad things there is a seed of good — Henry's habit of redeeming adversity
carefully extract the good — alchemical metaphor

glosses.phrase: bad neighbour makes us early stirrers
glosses.definition: a dangerous neighbour makes us rise early — the French force vigilance on Henry's men
good management; thriftiness
external conscience; moral reminders

glosses.phrase: dress us fairly for our end
glosses.definition: prepare ourselves properly for death
extract sweetness even from useless plants — proverbial optimism
Sir Thomas Erpingham — the old knight who commanded the English archers; a historical figure
learn to love current hardship — stoic endurance
shed skin (like a snake) and new nimbleness — image of renewal
Erpingham's cloak — Henry uses it to disguise himself as a common officer

glosses.phrase: I and my bosom must debate
glosses.definition: I and my heart must argue — solo self-examination
'God have mercy'; thanks
French: 'Who goes there?' — Pistol's attempt at a sentry's challenge
tell me — Pistolian inflation of 'say'
low-born, ordinary, or of the people
a gentleman attached to a company of soldiers — roughly officer-rank
do you carry the mighty pike? — a foot-soldier's weapon
fine fellow (French 'beau coq' — already glossed at 3.2)
offspring of fame — 'imp' = scion, noble graft
here a term of affection — 'fine fellow'; not yet the modern pejorative
Henry the King — Henry's French-disguised name; Pistol thinks it's Cornish
from Cornwall — 'le Roy' was a common Cornish surname

glosses.phrase: knock his leek about his pate
glosses.definition: smack his leek about his head — the leek is Fluellen's emblem, and Pistol promises to assault him with it; sets up 5.1
St. David's Day (March 1) — the Welsh national saint's day
obscene Spanish fig-gesture (already glossed in 3.6)
suits your aggressive name; goes well with your bluster
prerogatives, in Fluellen's accent — ancient military privileges
Pompey Magnus — Roman general and rival of Julius Caesar; Fluellen's model of military order
Fluellen's nonce-words for idle chatter
the French camp is noisy — contrasts with the quiet English
babbling fool — 'coxcomb' = fool's cap / fool

glosses.phrase: no great cause to desire the approach of day
glosses.definition: Bates doesn't look forward to dawn — the soldiers dread battle
Michael Williams — the soldier whose argument with Henry becomes the play's most sustained critique of kingship
shipwrecked on a sandbar — awaiting the next tide to sweep them off
shared his private fear

glosses.phrase: the king is but a man
glosses.definition: Henry's opening premise — the speech that attempts to demystify kingship to Williams and Bates

glosses.phrase: violet smells to him as it doth to me
glosses.definition: his senses are the same as ours — the body, not the office

glosses.phrase: element shows to him as it doth to me
glosses.definition: the sky looks the same to him as to me — 'element' = sky
set aside his ceremonial dignities
his passions fly higher — but they stoop like hawks, like ours
taste; quality
the courage one shows on the surface
immersed in the Thames — wishing himself at home, cold and safe
whatever the consequences; come what may
paid for his release — kings captured in battle were normally ransomed, not killed

glosses.phrase: his cause being just and his quarrel honourable
glosses.definition: Henry's insistence — the condition he keeps returning to; it is the condition Williams will challenge
Williams's decisive skeptical reply — the audience cannot verify royal justifications

glosses.phrase: obedience to the king wipes the crime of it out of us
glosses.definition: Bates's doctrine of passive obedience — our duty is to obey, not to judge the war's justice
if the war is not just
a terrible account to give (to God, on Judgment Day)
Judgment Day; the Last Judgment
children left destitute ('raw' = unprotected)

glosses.phrase: die well that die in a battle
glosses.definition: soldiers rarely die 'well' — i.e., in the state of Christian grace — since battle gives no time to settle affairs or repent
a serious moral indictment of the king — Williams holds Henry responsible

glosses.phrase: disobey were against all proportion of subjection
glosses.definition: yet refusing to fight would violate the duty of a subject — Williams sees the trap

glosses.phrase: son that is by his father sent about merchandise
glosses.definition: Henry's analogy — a son sent to trade who sins on the way; the father isn't damned for the son's sins
decision by combat; the judgment of battle
seduction under false promise of marriage
using the army as a refuge

glosses.phrase: defeated the law and outrun native punishment
glosses.definition: escaped civil justice at home
a minor parish officer who administered whippings; war as God's whip
spiritually unprepared; unconfessed

glosses.phrase: every subject’s soul is his own
glosses.definition: Henry's rhetorical pivot — the king answers for the justice of the war, not the state of each soul

glosses.phrase: wash every mote out of his conscience
glosses.definition: clean every speck from his conscience — a soldier's pre-battle spiritual hygiene
resolve to fight vigorously

glosses.phrase: king say he would not be ransomed
glosses.definition: Henry's pledge never to be captured — he fights or dies

glosses.phrase: when our throats are cut, he may be ransomed
glosses.definition: Williams's bitter skepticism — the dead can't check on the king's promises

glosses.phrase: perilous shot out of an elder-gun
glosses.definition: a dangerous pop from a pop-gun (elder-wood toy) — Williams mocks the commoner's leverage over the king
a feeble means — fanning the sun with a feather

glosses.phrase: Your reproof is something too round
glosses.definition: your rebuke is too direct

glosses.phrase: quarrel between us, if you live
glosses.definition: Williams proposes a personal duel after the battle if Henry survives — the glove plot begins
pledge of combat — typically a glove thrown down
soft cap
a slap on the side of the head — Williams's promised response to Henry's reappearance
20-to-1 odds; a French gold coin is also called a 'crown'
pun: clip gold coins (illegal coin-cutting) OR cut French heads — Henry's morbid joke
coin-clipper, but here the king himself will be 'clipping' French heads with the sword
the Old Testament God of Hosts — Henry's invocation before Agincourt
the counting of odds — Henry prays his men won't register how outnumbered they are

glosses.phrase: fault my father made in compassing the crown
glosses.definition: Henry IV's usurpation of Richard II's throne — the dynastic sin Henry V has inherited
Henry V reburied Richard II at Westminster Abbey (1413) — historical fact

glosses.phrase: Five hundred poor I have in yearly pay
glosses.definition: 500 poor people on annual stipend to pray for Richard
two chapels endowed for perpetual prayer — Henry's further atonement for his father's usurpation
my repentance comes after the deed — Henry knows his atonement can't truly erase the sin

annotations.title: Henry's philosophy of hardship — 'soul of goodness in things evil'
annotations.body: The scene opens with Henry's characteristic alchemical language: 'There is some soul of goodness in things evil, / Would men observingly distil it out.' The formulation is Stoic in its lineage — the philosopher's counsel that what cannot be avoided can be repurposed. Henry extends it: the 'bad neighbour' (the French army) is their 'outward conscience,' preaching 'that we should dress us fairly for our end.' What is unusual here is the private nature of the speech. Henry is speaking to his brothers and Erpingham before the sun is up; there is no crowd to impress, no audience to persuade. The speech is not performance; it is what the king actually thinks. This matters when we turn to the Chorus's heroic portrait and the Williams dialogue that follows: Henry genuinely believes hardship has spiritual value, and he genuinely believes a king's work is to model that belief to his men. The rhetorical question the play keeps asking is whether this private piety changes anything about the political and moral compromises Henry has already made.
sources: Harold Bloom, Shakespeare: The Invention of the Human (1998)

annotations.title: Henry's aside on Fluellen
annotations.body: In the brief moment after overhearing Fluellen rebuking Gower for loud speech at night, Henry says: 'Though it appear a little out of fashion, / There is much care and valour in this Welshman.' It is a small line, but important. Henry is watching a subordinate whom the audience has been permitted to find comic, and he sees something genuine in him. The line is evidence that Henry's generosity is not only rhetorical — and it anticipates Henry's public affirmation in 4.7 ('I am Welsh, you know, good countryman') that binds the king to the Welsh soldiery. A king who can hear out-of-fashion manners and still see care and valour in them is a king the play takes seriously. This is one of the small ways Shakespeare earns our ambivalence: not every Henry-moment is a calculation.
sources: Andrew Gurr (ed.), King Henry V (New Cambridge Shakespeare, 1992)
Peter Saccio, Shakespeare's English Kings (2nd ed., 2000)

annotations.title: The Williams debate — royal responsibility on trial
annotations.body: The dialogue between Henry (disguised) and the soldier Michael Williams is the play's most sustained philosophical passage. Williams's argument is precise: if the king's cause is unjust, his soldiers die damned; if they die damned, 'it will be a black matter for the king that led them to it.' Henry's reply is the long merchant/servant analogy (lines 122–145): a father who sends a son to sea does not damn himself if the son sins on the journey; a master who sends a servant with money is not damned if the servant is killed by robbers. The argument's logic is: each subject's soul is his own; the king is not responsible for the spiritual state of his soldiers at the moment of death. This is internally coherent theology. But it is also a careful evasion of Williams's real question, which is about the justness of the cause itself. Henry's reply does not engage the premise ('if the cause be not good'); it sidesteps it by asserting the separability of king's judgement and subject's soul. Williams's concession ('Ay, he said so, to make us fight cheerfully') suggests the soldier is not fully persuaded. The scene refuses to vindicate either voice. It is the play's most direct staging of the critique that the Chorus's rhetoric wants to suppress.

annotations.title: 'Not today, O Lord' — Henry IV's usurpation and the guilty crown
annotations.body: Henry's prayer alone before dawn — 'Not today, O Lord, / O, not today, think not upon the fault / My father made in compassing the crown' — is one of the play's most historically rich moments. Henry IV had seized the throne from his cousin Richard II in 1399, forcing Richard's abdication and later condoning his murder. This dynastic sin has shadowed the Lancastrian line throughout the first tetralogy (Henry VI Parts 1–3) and the second (Richard II, Henry IV Parts 1–2). Henry V's reburial of Richard II at Westminster Abbey (historically attested, 1413) and his endowment of two chantries for Richard's soul are acts of atonement specifically. Henry's prayer is honest about this: 'all that I can do is nothing worth, / Since that my penitence comes after all.' The Lancastrian curse — that usurpation poisons the crown and will eventually bring civil war — is acknowledged by the king himself in solitude. The Epilogue will return to it: Henry VI will lose France and England will bleed in the Wars of the Roses that end the tetralogy Shakespeare had already written.
sources: Peter Saccio, Shakespeare's English Kings (2nd ed., 2000)

trials.prompt: What philosophical stance opens Henry's speech to his brothers?
options: Pure fatalism

A stoic/alchemical stance: 'some soul of goodness in things evil' — hardship can be distilled into spiritual preparation; the bad neighbour is an 'outward conscience' teaching us to 'dress us fairly for our end'
Scepticism about prayer
A renunciation of kingship

trials.prompt: What is Williams's central argument about royal responsibility?
options: That kings cannot be challenged under any circumstances

That if the king's cause is unjust, his soldiers die in a state of unrepented sin — and therefore 'it will be a black matter for the king that led them to it.' The king's moral accountability is load-bearing
That soldiers should refuse to fight without pay
That Henry is a kind king and deserves no criticism

trials.prompt: How does Henry answer Williams's challenge?
options: By agreeing entirely

With the father/son and master/servant analogies: each subject's soul is his own; the king isn't damned for the private state of each soldier. The reply is theologically coherent but sidesteps Williams's actual question about the justice of the war
By threatening to have him executed
By revealing his identity as king on the spot

trials.prompt: What 'fault' does Henry pray God to 'think not upon' at line 198?
options: His own drunken youth
His father Henry IV's usurpation of Richard II's throne in 1399 — the dynastic sin Henry V has tried to atone for by reburying Richard and endowing chantries for his soul
The tennis-balls insult
The death of Falstaff

trials.prompt: What does the glove-exchange between Henry and Williams set up?
options: Nothing — it is a throwaway moment
A two-scene comic pay-off: Henry will give the glove to Fluellen in 4.7, Williams will punch Fluellen in 4.8, and Henry will reveal the trick and reward Williams with a glove full of crowns
A duel that actually happens during Agincourt
An engagement between Williams and Alice
